coredata.dc:description: Metal dyshomeostasis is one of the pathological features of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Cu2+ is believed to induce the formation of neurotoxic β-amyloid (Aβ) aggregates and aggravate oxidative damage. It is challenging to take effective therapeutic interventions towards the complicated pathogenesis related to Cu2+. Herein, chiral carbon dots were proposed for Cu2+-related AD therapy. The chiral CDs were prepared based on the ternary carbon sources, using L/d-cysteine as the chiral carbon source, citric acid as the carbonization source and β-cyclodextrin as the chirality inducer, reserving and enhancing the chirality of CDs. Compared to L-Cys-CDs, D-Cys-CDs exhibited enhanced chirality, stronger affinity towards Cu2+ and comparable antioxidant ability, but better inhibition on peroxidase-like activity of Cu2+ or Aβ42-Cu2+. Thus, D-Cys-CDs performed better to inhibit Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation. Meanwhile, D-Cys-CDs can alleviate the cytotoxicity of Aβ42-Cu2+ with satisfactory anti-inflammation effect and scavenging ability towards intracellular reactive oxygen species. D-Cys-CDs succeeded in reducing Aβ deposits and releasing paralysis for the AD model CL2120 nematodes. D-Cys-CDs with an enantiomeric effect on Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation and the peroxidase-like activity of Cu2+ was promising as the multifunctional therapeutic agent for Cu2+-induced AD therapy.

(E) The excitation and emission spectra of L-Cys-CDs. Inset: L-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL) under natural (left) and UV (λex = 365 nm) (right) light. The inhibitory effects of Cys-CDs on Aβ42 aggregation. (A) Effects of Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL) on pure Aβ42 aggregation by ThT assay. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). The white arrows indicated the hydrogen bonds with Gly9 and His13 residue of Cu-Aβ1-23 for D-Cys. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Scavenging of free radicals by Cys-CDs. Elimination rates towards (A) •OH and (B) O2 •- by Cys-CDs of different concentrations. Regulation effects of POD-like activity of Aβ42-Cu2+ by Cys-CDs. (C) Absorbance change (A652) of ox-TMB over time on the TMB assay catalyzed by Cu2+ (20 μM). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). (D) Absorbance change (A652) of ox-TMB over time on the TMB assay catalyzed by (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (20 μM). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Cellular uptake and the anti-inflammation test. (B) Flow cytometric analysis of SH-SY5Y cells after treatment with L/D-Cys-Cy5 (200 μg/mL) using untreated cells as control. (C) Relative mRNA level of NFKBIA of SH-SY5Y cells treated with L/D-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL) and stimulated by (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (20 μM) or not (Control). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). **P < 0.01, n.s., not significant. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). ***P < 0.001, n.s., not significant. (E) Relative mRNA level of IL-6 of SH-SY5Y cells treated with L/D-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL) and stimulated by (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (20 μM) or not (Control). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Scavenging intracellular ROS by Cys-CDs. (A) Fluorescence and bright field images of SH-SY5Y cells treated by L/D-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL) and stimulated by (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (20 μM) or not (Control). Scale bar = 25 μm. (B) The average fluorescence of DCF of SH-SY5Y cells of (A) calculated by Image J. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). ***P < 0.001. (C) Fluorescence and bright field images of SH-SY5Y cells treated by L/D-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL) and stimulated by H2O2 (100 μM) or not (Control). Scale bar = 25 μm. (B) Alleviation effects of L/D-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL) on (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (20 μM) induced cytotoxicity of SH-SY5Y cells. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(B) Average fluorescence intensity of (A) calculated by Image J. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). *P < 0.05, n.s., not significant. head.ce:title: Chiral carbon dots based on ternary carbon sources: A multifunctional therapeutic agent for Cu2+-induced Alzheimer's disease

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal dyshomeostasis is one of the pathological features of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Cu2+ is believed to induce the formation of neurotoxic β-amyloid (Aβ) aggregates and aggravate oxidative damage. It is challenging to take effective therapeutic interventions towards the complicated pathogenesis related to Cu2+. Herein, chiral carbon dots were proposed for Cu2+-related AD therapy. The chiral CDs were prepared based on the ternary carbon sources, using L/d-cysteine as the chiral carbon source, citric acid as the carbonization source and β-cyclodextrin as the chirality inducer, reserving and enhancing the chirality of CDs. Compared to L-Cys-CDs, D-Cys-CDs exhibited enhanced chirality, stronger affinity towards Cu2+ and comparable antioxidant ability, but better inhibition on peroxidase-like activity of Cu2+ or Aβ42-Cu2+. Thus, D-Cys-CDs performed better to inhibit Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation. Meanwhile, D-Cys-CDs can alleviate the cytotoxicity of Aβ42-Cu2+ with satisfactory anti-inflammation effect and scavenging ability towards intracellular reactive oxygen species. D-Cys-CDs succeeded in reducing Aβ deposits and releasing paralysis for the AD model CL2120 nematodes. D-Cys-CDs with an enantiomeric effect on Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation and the peroxidase-like activity of Cu2+ was promising as the multifunctional therapeutic agent for Cu2+-induced AD therapy.

The enantiomeric effects of the chiral carbon dots on inhibition of Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation, regulation of the peroxidase-like activity of Cu2+, protection of SH-SY5Y cells and C.elegans 2120 were investigated.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is one of the most prevalent dementia, characterized by irreversible and progressive cognitive dysfunction [1]. Senile plaques, also known as amyloid plaques which are composed of insoluble and aggregative peptides, e.g. β-amyloid protein (Aβ), are well recognized as the pathogenesis of AD [2]. These self-assembly peptide aggregates produce extracellular deposition or smaller soluble oligomer species, recognized as the amyloid cascade hypothesis or oligomer hypothesis, respectively [3]. In addition to these two well-accepted theories, recently, metal dyshomeostasis has unquestionably gained much attention [4]. Substantial quantities of metal ions, including Cu2+, Fe3+ and Zn2+, are found in the core and periphery of plaque formations in the brains of AD patients [5]. Among them, Cu2+ is generally believed to play a role in two pathways relevant to the pathophysiology of AD. One is that Cu2+ would bind to three histidine units (i.e. His6, His13 and His14) of Aβ peptide, resulting in the formation of Aβ-Cu2+ complex; the aggregation of Aβ would be further induced by Cu2+ or Aβ-Cu2+ [6]. The other is that Cu2+ or Aβ-Cu2+ complex could catalyze the generation of neurotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as oxygen-containing free radicals and H2O2, which would exacerbate oxidative stress, and cause DNA damage and mitochondrial dysfunction triggered by Aβ-Cu2+ [7].

Given the critical role of Cu2+, many attempts have been made to regulate Cu2+ or Aβ-Cu2+ complex using a metal-protein attenuating substance with a high affinity towards Cu2+ for AD therapy [6,8]. However, metal chelators with only single functions were not effective enough to stop AD progress due to complicated pathologies involved in AD such as oxidative stress damage caused by excessive ROS in addition to metal dyshomeostasis [9,10]. Thus, it is urgent to develop multifunctional agents targeting multiple pathologies of AD, not just for inhibition of Aβ aggregation or chelation of excess Cu2+, but also for scavenging ROS and anti-neuroinflammation [11].

Chirality is a universally observed but highly compelling phenomenon in nature. Chirality has been noticed to exert different effects on alleviating AD symptoms. For example, chiral liposomes modified with aspartic acid enantiomers exhibited distinct inhibitory effects on the fibrillation of Aβ, probably due to the different electrostatic interactions between L/d-aspartic acid and Aβ monomer [12]. Another example was that the d-glutathione stabilized gold nanoparticles with a larger binding affinity to Aβ demonstrated superior efficacy to their enantiomer in rescuing behavioral impairment in AD model mice [13]. Despite promising findings regarding the potential of chiral agents for AD therapy, numerous unanswered questions still remain, e.g. how does the chiral precursor build into chiral material at the nanoscale; how does chiral material influence Aβ aggregation and Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation; whether does enhanced chirality benefit AD therapy or not, etc. Particularly, the chiral effect on Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation has been seldom researched yet.

In this study, we proposed a kind of multifunctional carbon dots (CDs), named D-Cys-CDs with chirality at the nanoscale, Cu2+-binding affinity and free radical scavenging ability, for AD therapy. CDs are one type of appealing nanoagents with rich functional groups on their surface for interacting with proteins and cells [14–16]. Chiral CDs enantiomers can interact with proteins differently and thus regulate the intracellular process differently, holding the potential to target and treat specific diseases. In an attempt to regulate the hydrolytic rate of maltose, D-glutamic-derived CDs were utilized for the modification of maltase with a better inhibition ratio against maltase than L-glutamic-derived ones [17]. Importantly, chiral CDs synthesized from l-lysine were reported to modulate Aβ fibrillation better than its enantiomeric ones [18].

Inspired by the above studies, herein, we attempted to construct chiral CDs to regulate Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation. The cysteine-derived CDs (Cys-CDs), i.e. D-Cys-CDs and L-Cys-CDs, were constructed using D/l-cysteine (Cys) as the chiral carbon source and β-cyclodextrin as the chirality inducer. Compared to L-Cys-CDs, D-Cys-CDs exerted a significant inhibitory effect on Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation due to its high chirality and binding affinity to Cu2+. Meanwhile, although D-Cys-CDs acted as an excellent scavenger as L-Cys-CDs towards free radicals, D-Cys-CDs regulated peroxidase (POD)-like activity of Cu2+ and Aβ42-Cu2+ much better, and further promoted mitigating excess ROS produced by Cu2+ and Aβ42-Cu2+. Additionally, D-Cys-CDs can reduce cytotoxicity induced by Aβ42-Cu2+ with a good anti-inflammation effect. Owing to these appealing properties, D-Cys-CDs succeeded in inhibiting the accumulation and deposition of Aβ42 in CL2120 nematodes and relieving their paralysis. This study provides a preliminary experimental basis for constructing multifunctional chiral CDs for Cu2+-related AD therapy.

Citric acid (CA), HCl, KCl, NaOH, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O, CuCl2, H2O2 (30 % aqueous solution), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). L-Cys, D-Cys, Thioflavin T (ThT), methylene blue (MB), nitro blue tetrazolium chloride monohydrate (NBT), riboflavin, dichlorodihydrofluorescein-diacetate (DCFH-DA), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were obtained from the Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). β-cyclodextrin was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Japan). Sulfo-cyanine5 (Cy5) and 5-fluoro-2-deoxyuridine (FUDR) were obtained from Macklin Inc. (Shanghai, China). Aβ42 was purchased from the Lifetein Biotechnology (Beijing, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) was obtained from Gibco (New York, USA). Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was purchased from the Biosharp Life Sciences (Hefei, China). NucleoZol and NovoScrip Plus were purchased from Takara (Japan) and Novoprotein (Nanjing, China), respectively. Ultrapure water was produced by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China).

The CDs were synthesized according to the previous work with minor modifications via a simple microwave-assisted heating approach [19]. Generally, 0.19 g of CA, 0.12 g of L-Cys or D-Cys, and a certain amount of β-cyclodextrin as listed in Table S1 were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. The solution was heated in a domestic microwave oven at a power of 750 W for 9 min, and the water was evaporated during the procedure. After cooling down to room temperature, the crude product was dispersed to 5 mL of deionized water as a brown solution, and subjected to dialysis for 48 h in a 3500 Da dialysis bag to remove small molecules by renewing the dialysate (deionized water) every 6–8 h. The solution inside the dialysis bag was then collected and named as in Table S1. Racemic mixture of L/D-CA-CDs and L/D-Cys-CDs were prepared by simply mixing two enantiomeric pairs at the same concentration, named as rac-CA-CDs and rac-Cys-CDs, respectively.

The transmission electric microscopic (TEM) images were captured by a Talos F200X electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained on an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted by an ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fischer, USA) with an X-ray source of A1 Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) under a base pressure of 8 × 10−10 Pa. Fluorescence spectra were acquired by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and fluorescence images were observed by an inverted fluorescence microscopy (Olympus, Germany). The UV absorption spectra were recorded by a Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Circular dichroism spectra were collected by an MOS-500 circular dichroism spectrometer (Bio-Logic, France). The absorbance of samples in 96-well plates was recorded by a Synergy HT multi-functional microplate reader (BioTek, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of binding constant (Kb) and log P values
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding constant K
b between CDs and Cu2+ was studied by fluorescence titration. Typically, the specific CDs were co-incubated with Cu2+ of different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000 μM) in HEPES (10 mM, pH 7.4) for 20 min, and then the fluorescence intensity was determined (λex = 350 nm, λem = 425 nm). K
b was calculated from Eq. (1) based on the Benesi-Hildebrand plot.

Where I and I
0 are the fluorescence intensity of the studied CDs with or without Cu2+, respectively; I max is the saturated fluorescence intensity; [Cu2+] is the concentration of Cu2+; K
b is calculated as intercept/slope from the fitting plot of 1/(I
0 –I) versus 1/[Cu2+].

The log P values were determined as octanol-water partition coefficient by the shake-flask method. Firstly, water and octanol were mutually saturated before use. Then, 1 mL of octanol saturated with water mixed with 1 mg of CDs was added to the Eppendorf tube (EP tube), followed by the addition of 3 mL of water saturated with octanol. The mixture was shaken by a shaking incubator at 25 °C for 24 h. After that, the mixture was placed still until phase separation. The octanol phase and water phase were separately sucked out carefully, and the concentration of the four CDs in the two phases was determined by the UV–vis measurement. The log P was calculated from Eq. (2) .
(2)
log
P = lg
(
C o
/
C w
)
where C
o and C
w were the concentrations of CDs in the octanol and water, respectively.

Aβ42 powder was firstly dissolved in 60 mM NaOH aqueous solution as an Aβ42 stock solution of 200 μM. The Aβ42 stock solution was diluted with phosphate-buffered saline (50 mM PBS containing 100 mM NaCl, pH 7.4) to 20 μM, and added with 8 μM CuCl2, incubating at room temperature for 10 min to form the complex in an Aβ42 to Cu2+ ratio of 2.5 : 1 (mol: mol), named as (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5. To demonstrate that Cu2+ was successfully bound to Aβ42, the prepared Aβ42-Cu2+ was subjected to dialysis, and Cu2+ in the dialysate was analyzed by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Similarly, the equimolar complex of Aβ42 and Cu2+ (200 μM) (1 : 1, mol: mol) was prepared using 2 mM of the Aβ42 stock solution, named as (Aβ42-Cu2+)1.

To promote amyloid aggregation and obtain the fibrils of Aβ42-Cu2+, (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5 was incubated in 37 °C PBS (50 mM containing 100 mM NaCl, pH 7.4) for 6 h [20]. Firstly, Cu2+ and Cys-CDs did not affect the fluorescence of ThT under our experimental condition (Fig. S1). To investigate the inhibition ability of CDs on Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation, six groups of experiments were carried out: (I) Aβ42 alone (20 μM); (II) Aβ42 (20 μM) co-incubated with L-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL); (III) Aβ42 (20 μM) co-incubated with D-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL); (IV) (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5; (V) (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5 co-incubated with L-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL); (VI) (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5 co-incubated with D-Cys-CDs (200 μg/mL) in 37 °C PBS (50 mM containing 100 mM NaCl, pH 7.4) for 6 h.

The ThT assay: The aliquot of the above incubated solutions (20 μL) was sampled at the prescribed time interval and added with ThT (25 μM) in PB (10 mM, pH 7.4) to the final volume of 1 mL, and then subjected to the fluorescence measurement (λex = 430 nm, λem = 488 nm). The normalized fluorescence intensity was calculated using the maximum fluorescence of the group (I) and (IV) as respective 100 %. The aggregation kinetics was fitted as Boltzmann equation (Eq. 3):
(3)
Y =
Y 0
+
(
Y max
−
Y 0
)
/

1 + exp −
(
t −
t
1 / 2
)

]
where Ymax refers to the maximum fluorescence of each incubation group; Y0 refers to fluorescence intensity at the initial time (0 h); t1/2 refers to the time when the normalized fluorescence intensity reaches 50 % of the maximum fluorescence; k refers to the elongation rate constant.

The TEM observation: Briefly, 10 μL samples at the ending point of the above groups (I, IV, V, VI) were collected and then pipetted onto a copper grid. After 5 min, the copper grid was washed with deionized water three times and dropped by the freshly prepared aqueous solution of 2 % phosphotungstic acid for negative staining. Finally, thus-prepared samples were subjected to the TEM observation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ABTS+•, Fenton reaction, TMB and NBT assays, and intracellular ROS scavenging by Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ABTS+• assay: The single electron of ABTS+• could be captured by the free radical scavenger, resulting in the weakened absorbance at 731 nm (A731). Briefly, 7 mM aqueous solution of ABTS was mixed with 2.45 mM K2S2O8 aqueous solution, and kept still in the dark at room temperature for 16 h. 190 μL of the solution was then added with 10 μL of specific CDs of different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600 μg/mL) with incubation for 30 min, and A731 was recorded.

The Fenton reaction assay: MB with the obvious absorption centering at 654 nm (A654) was used to capture •OH produced by the Fenton reaction. When the •OH scavenger is present, the strengthened A654 can be recorded to quantify •OH scavenging activity. To investigate the •OH scavenging ability of Cys-CDs, 150 μL of the •OH solution which was prepared by mixing 1.2 mM FeSO4 and 1 mM MB, was added with 40 μL of L/D-Cys-CDs of different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600 μg/mL) and 10 μL of H2O2 aqueous solution (20 mM). After incubating at room temperature for 30 min, A654 was measured.

The NBT assay: NBT can be oxidized by O2 •- generated from riboflavin by irradiation to have the absorbance at 620 nm (A620). Typically, 20 μL of NBT (4.5 mM), 20 μL of methionine (130 mM), 20 μL of riboflavin (20 μM), and 20 μL of Cys-CDs of different concentrations (0, 50, 200, 400, 600 μg/mL) were mixed in PBS (50 mM, pH 7.8). Then, the mixtures were irradiated with a light-emitting diode lamp for 2 min. The A620 was recorded by a microplate reader.

The elimination rates of the above three free radicals, i.e. ABTS+•, •OH and O2 •-, by Cys-CDs were all calculated according to the following Eq. (4) :
(4)
Elimination
rate
( % )
=
[
1 −
(
A −
A 0
)
/
A 1
]
× 100 %
where A is the absorbance of the above respective three systems with specific free radical and Cys-CDs, i.e. A731, A652 and A620 for ABTS+•, •OH and O2 •-; A0 represents the absorbance of the specific CDs solution alone; A1 refers to the absorbance of free radical solution without CDs.

The TMB assay: TMB is oxidized by •OH generated from H2O2, which can be catalyzed by Cu2+, to blue ox-TMB with absorbance at 652 nm (A652). If the POD-like catalytic activity of Cu2+ was inhibited, the produced •OH would be decreased and thus A652 would be weakened. Herein, the TMB assay was carried out to determine whether the specific CDs affected the POD-like catalytic activity of Cu2+. Briefly, TMB was dissolved in DMSO and diluted to 100 μg/mL in NaOAc-HOAc buffer (100 mM, pH 5). Then, 150 μL of the above TMB solution (100 μg/mL) and 10 μL of H2O2 aqueous solution (200 mM) were mixed, followed by the addition of 20 μL of CuCl2 aqueous solution (200 μM) or (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (200 μM), and 20 μL of specific CDs to different final concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600 μg/mL). A652 was monitored at the time points of 0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 1.5h, 2.0 h, 3.0 h, 4.0 h and 6.0 h. The above assay was carried out without Cu2+ to test the POD activity of L/D-Cys-CDs.

Intracellular ROS scavenging: Human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells (the China Center for Type Culture Collection, Wuhan, China) were incubated in DMEM containing 8 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, and then seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 8000 cells/well with incubation for 12 h. After the medium was removed, SH-SY5Y cells were incubated in the presence of (Aβ4 2-Cu2+)1 (20 μM) or H2O2 (100 μM) and specific CDs (200 μg/mL) for 4 h. Then, cells were gently washed with DMEM twice and stained with freshly-prepared DCFH-DA solution (10 μM) for another 30 min. After being washed 3 times with DMEM, SH-SY5Y cells were imaged by a fluorescence microscope and the fluorescence intensity of each image was quantified by Image J (1.52a).

SH-SY5Y cells were incubated in DMEM containing 8 % FBS at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, and then seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 8000 cells/well with incubation for 12 h. The (Aβ4 2-Cu2+)1 pre-incubated in 37 °C PBS (50 mM PBS containing 100 mM NaCl, pH 7.4) for 2 h, and specific CDs of 200 μg/mL were added into wells with incubation for another 24 h. Then, SH-SY5Y cells were gently washed by DMEM three times, and followed by the addition of CCK-8 (10 % DMEM, v/v) with incubation for 1 h. The cell viability was calculated based on Eq. (5) by measuring the absorbance of each well (λ = 450 nm).

ODtreated and ODcontrol are the absorbance at 450 nm for the cells treated with specific CDs and not treated with CDs, respectively. ODCCK-8 is the absorbance of CCK-8 (10 % DMEM, v/v).

SH-SY5Y cells were incubated in DMEM containing 8 % FBS at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, and then seeded in a 6-well plate at a density of 5 × 105 cells/well with incubation for 16 h. L/D-Cys-CDs of 200 μg/mL were added into the well with incubation for another 24 h. Afterward, SH-SY5Y cells were gently washed with DMEM three times and incubated with (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (20 μM) in DMEM for 9 h. After that, the total mRNA was extracted from SH-SY5Y cells using the NucleoZol reagent. The reverse transcription was applied using NovoScrip Plus. Real-time qPCR analysis was undertaken using SYBR Premix Ex Taq [21].

Conjugation of Cys-CDs with Cy5: 5 μL of Cy5 (5 mg/mL, DMSO) was diluted in 500 μL of PB (50 mM, pH 6.0) with the addition of EDC (25 mg) and NHS (25 mg) by gently shaking for 30 min. After that, Cys-CDs (20 mg/mL) were mixed with this Cy5 solution in the volume ratio of 1 : 1. After incubation of 4 h, the mixture solution was transferred into a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove small molecules by renewing the dialysate (deionized water) every 4 h for 1 day. Finally, the dialyzed solution containing the Cy5 conjugated Cys-CDs, named as L/D-Cys-CDs-Cy5, was stored at 4 °C for further use. The successful conjugation of L/D-Cys-CDs-Cy5 was confirmed by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (λex = 660 nm) (Fig. S2).

Confocal fluorescence microscopic observation: SH-SY5Y cells were incubated in DMEM containing 8 % FBS at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, and then seeded on circular cell climbing films in a 24-well plate at a density of 4 × 104 cells/well with incubation for 16 h. Then the cells were co-incubated with L/D-Cys-CDs-Cy5 (200 μg/mL) in the fresh DMEM containing 8 % FBS. After incubation of 4 h, the cells were washed with PBS three times and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min. Then, cells were washed three times, and stained by DAPI (1 μg/mL) for another 15 min. The circular cell climbing films were collected and subjected to a Nikon AX confocal fluorescence microscope under fluorescence modes (Nikon, Japan) for observation with a prolonging anti-quenching agent (DAPI: λex = 405 nm, Cy5: λex = 650 nm). The fluorescence images were analyzed by Image J.

Flow cytometric analysis: SH-SY5Y cells were incubated in DMEM containing 8 % FBS at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, and seeded on circular cell climbing films in a 6-well plate at a density of 5 × 105 cells/well with incubation for 16 h. Then the cells were co-incubated with L/D-Cys-CDs-Cy5 (200 μg/mL) in fresh DMEM containing 8 % FBS for 4 h. Afterwards, the cells were washed by PBS three times and then collected for flow cytometric analysis (λem = 675 nm, slit width = 25 nm) (BD Accuri C6, USA). The cells without any treatment acted as the control.

Worm strain and maintenance: The CL2120 nematodes (American Type Culture Collection, USA) were maintained on the NGM agar plates with Escherichia coli strain OP50 as food. The nematodes were washed by M9 buffer (containing 22 mM KH2PO4, 42.3 mM Na2HPO4, 85.5 mM NaCl and 1 mM MgSO4) and bleached by hypochlorite bleaching buffer (containing 1 M NaOH and 5.9 M NaClO) to collect the embryos. The collected embryos were divided into six groups with treating by (a) none (control), (b) Cu2+ (60 μM), (c) L-Cys-CDs (600 μg/mL), (d) L-Cys-CDs (600 μg/mL) and Cu2+ (60 μM), (e) D-Cys-CDs (600 μg/mL), (f) D-Cys-CDs (600 μg/mL) and Cu2+ (60 μM). They were transferred onto OP50/NGM plates with incubation at 20 °C to obtain synchronized 4th stage Larve.

Fluorescence imaging of Aβ deposit: the 4th stage Larve worms on the 3rd day of adulthood were collected and fixed with levamisole (10 mM in M9 buffer) to avoid moving during observation. The anesthetic worms were observed by the fluorescence microscope, and the fluorescence intensity of each image was quantified by Image J.

The paralysis assay: 30 worms of the above synchronized 4th stage Larve in each group were transferred onto the new plates with Escherichia coli strain OP50 and cultured at 25 °C. To prevent self-production of C. elegans, the FUDR (100 mM) was added to each plate on the first day of adulthood [22]. The worms were tested for paralysis by tapping their heads with a platinum wire. Worms that moved their heads but failed to move their bodies were scored as “paralyzed”. Each group of survived worms was subjected to this test every 3 days after adulthood [23]. To avoid scoring aged worms as paralyzed by mistake, the paralysis assay was terminated on the 9th day of adulthood.

To explore the interactions among CA, L/D-Cys and β-cyclodextrin, their geometric structures were optimized using the density functional theory method at the B3LYP/6-31G(d) level using Gaussian 09 program (Revision 01). Binding calculations were conducted among CA, L/D-Cys and β-cyclodextrin by Auto Dock Tool 4.2.6. All torsion angles for CA and L/D-Cys were considered flexible. A grid of β-cyclodextrin was constructed with sufficient space to fully encapsulate the entire molecule.

To explore the interaction between L/D-Cys-CD and Cu-Aβ complex, the structure of Cu-Aβ1-23 (PDB, 8B9R) was obtained from the Protein Data Bank. Considering that CDs had unknown definite structures but reserved some residues from their carbon sources, L/D-Cys were adopted as alternatives to respective L/D-Cys-CD to carry out docking study. Binding calculations were conducted between L/D-Cys and Cu-Aβ1-23 by Auto Dock Tool. A grid of Cu-Aβ1-23 was constructed with sufficient space to cover the entire molecule. For accurate calculations, the exhaustiveness for the docking runs was set to 30 iterations while keeping other parameters at their default values. All docked models were visualized using Pymol.

Statistical analyses were performed by one-way ANOVA and represented as mean ± SD. Experiments were performed in triple unless stated otherwise. Differences were considered statistically significant at *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001.

Cys is reducible in nature, but unstable as it is sensitive to air for its nucleophilic thiol group [24]. Carbonization of Cys may enhance its stability while retaining its antioxidant activity. In previous reports, the CDs using Cys as the carbon source possessed a good fluorescence response towards Cu2+, as they reserved functional groups of Cys and were endowed with an affinity for Cu2+ [19]. Hence, they were highly expected as nanoagents to modulate Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation and the POD-like activity of Cu2+ [25]. Also, the carbonization of Cys may induce new functional groups into CDs, enabling specific interactions with free radicals. As a result, the CDs could exert ROS scavenging ability, which is also essential for AD therapy [26]. Additionally, Cys is a common enantiomeric carbon source for generating chiral CDs [27]. Enantiomeric nanoagents could exhibit distinct biological effects, and this was also the case for AD therapy. As reported, the CDs derived from l-lysine affected the fibrillation of Aβ42 more prominently than its enantiomeric ones, thus exhibiting better inhibition on the cytotoxic Aβ42 than d-lysine-CDs [18]. The enantiomeric Au nanoparticles obtained from L/D-dipeptide and gold seeds exhibited substantial differences in immune responses in vitro and in vivo. The left-handed nanoparticles exhibited superior efficacy to their right-handed counterparts as adjuvants in vaccination, primarily attributed to chirality-dependent variances in endocytosis by immune cells [28]. Based on the above considerations, herein, both D- or L-Cys were separately employed as the carbon source to generate chiral CDs to explore their difference in terms of affinity for Cu2+ and ROS scavenging ability. In addition, for better carbonization, CA assisting carbonization and nucleation was simultaneously adopted as another carbon source.

On the other hand, β-cyclodextrin, well known for its recognition of chiral compounds via the host-guest interaction, can act as the chiral inducer for constructing optical active materials [29]. Based on this knowledge, β-cyclodextrin was deliberately used as the third carbon source to promote the chirality of Cys-CDs. As listed in Table S1, L-CA-CDs and D-CA-CDs without β-cyclodextrin were also prepared in the same way for comparison. As recorded in Fig. 1 A, the circular dichroism spectra reflected that all the four CDs had chirality. Compared to the raw material of D-Cys, D-CA-CDs and D-Cys-CDs had opposite intensities at 206 nm, whereas the typical cotton effect of D-Cys exists. The circular dichroism of D-Cys was reversed in D-CA-CDs, but retained in D-Cys-CDs. In addition to the weak peak at 206 nm indicating the heritage of chirality from D-Cys, another new strong peak in the range of 220–300 nm appeared for all the four CDs, which may be introduced by the π–π* conjugation of sp2-hybridized carbon in the carbonized core of CDs, implying that the optical activity of chiral CDs may also originate from the formation of CDs [30]. Thus, CA-CDs and Cys-CDs both possessed more than one asymmetric center. Additionally, compared to CA-CDs, the Cys-CDs exhibited a more pronounced intensity within the 220–300 nm range, disclosing the stronger optical activity of Cys-CDs. Considering that β-cyclodextrin itself does not possess chirality while the produced Cys-CDs possessed obviously enhanced chirality and reserved the chiral center from the Cys, we deduced that this observation may be related to different interactions between the ternary carbon sources (as discussed below). Nevertheless, the chirality of rac-CA-CDs and rac-Cys-CDs was reduced (Fig. S3). Meanwhile, the circular dichroism spectra of rac-CA-CDs produced flat and achiral signal within the 220–300 nm range, while rac-Cys-CDs still had positive signal.

As the constructed Cys-CDs were supposed to have antioxidant activity and binding affinity towards Cu2+, to figure out the possible multiple roles of β-cyclodextrin contributing to these properties of Cys-CDs, the ABTS+• assay and fluorescence titration were performed. As listed in Table S1, there was no obvious difference in ABTS+• elimination rates between the enantiomeric pairs, i.e. L/D-Cys-CDs (∼70 %) and L/D-CA-CDs (∼40 %). However, the pair of L/D-Cys-CDs exerted remarkably higher ABTS+• elimination rates than those of the L/D-CA-CDs pair, possibly indicating that the joint usage of β-cyclodextrin as another carbon source may enhance the total antioxidant activity of Cys-CDs. The abundant hydroxy of β-cyclodextrin may be reserved in Cys-CDs (as discussed in Section 3.2), enhancing their dispersibility and hydrophilicity. This was reflected by log P values (Table S1), and the L/D-CA-CDs pair had higher log P values than those of L/D-Cys-CDs. Thereby, the interactions between Cys-CDs and free radicals would be promoted as a result of better contact, thus leading to elevated antioxidant activity [31]. As depicted in Fig. S4, both L/D-Cys-CDs possessed comparable excellent scavenging abilities to eliminate ABTS+• in a concentration-dependent manner with the EC50 of 200 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL, respectively. However, in this regard, the L/D-Cys-CDs pair behaved similarly to each other and had no enantiomeric effect. The comparable ABTS+• elimination rates and log P values of the racemic mixture, i.e.rac-CA-CDs and rac-Cys-CDs, also stood for this.

To investigate whether β-cyclodextrin contributed to the interaction of Cys-CDs with Cu2+, the fluorescence titration spectra of the four CDs with Cu2+ were recorded and compared in Fig. S5. Obviously, the fluorescence of the four CDs was all quenched by Cu2+ in a concentration-dependent way, i.e. the fluorescence weakened with the elevated concentration of Cu2+, but the quenched degree varied among the four CDs. Taking 500 μM Cu2+ as an example, the fluorescence was quenched by about 69 %, 46 %, 30 % and 13 % for D-Cys-CDs, L-Cys-CDs, L-CA-CDs and D-CA-CDs in this order, possibly implying their different affinities towards Cu2+. As a demonstration (Table S1), D-Cys-CDs and Cu2+ had the largest K
b , about 20-fold higher than L/D-CA-CDs and their racemic mixture, 1.36-fold higher than L-Cys-CDs and 45-fold higher than rac-Cys-CDs. These data illustrate that chirality may be essential for the Cu2+-binding affinity of D-Cys-CDs. Further, the impact of pH on the binding constant K
b between Cys-CDs and Cu2+ was studied in Fig. S6. The binding constants were determined in a narrowed pH range of 7.2–7.6 close to physiological environment (Fig. S6). As can be seen, the binding constants remained almost constant during pH of 7.2–7.6, indicating physiological pH had less influence on their binding affinity.

Based on the above discussions, β-cyclodextrin could play crucial roles in inducing the chirality, enhancing antioxidant properties and affinity (to Cu2+) of the Cys-CDs. Cys and β-cyclodextrin could form the inclusion complex [32]. As disclosed by the docking study in Fig. S7, both L-Cys and D-Cys can be entrapped in the cavity of β-cyclodextrin with two hydrogen bonding sites, while CA also exhibits comparable interactions with β-cyclodextrin (Fig. S7A-I, S7A-II and S7A-III). Thus, in the case of the co-existence of CA and Cys, competition between them for entering the cavity of β-cyclodextrin may occur, leading to different interactions between these raw materials [33]. The docking study revealed that L-Cys tend to form hydrogen bonds preferably with the other guest molecule CA instead of β-cyclodextrin (Fig. S7B-Ӏ). In contrast, D-Cys could form hydrogen bonds with β-cyclodextrin through carbonyl and amino groups (Fig. S7B-II). Hence, β-cyclodextrin would form hydrogen bonds differently with L/D-Cys in the presence of CA, and may provide different microenvironments for carbonization. As a result, higher affinity for Cu2+ and obvious chirality of D-Cys-CDs were obtained.

L/D-Cys-CDs were further characterized by TEM, UV and fluorescence spectra, FT-IR, and XPS. The morphology and particle size distribution of L/D-Cys-CDs were displayed in Fig. 1B and C. Both the two L/D-Cys-CDs appeared as quasi-sphere of sizes less than 2 nm with narrowed size distribution (Fig. 1B and C, Insets), 1.4 ± 0.4 nm for L-Cys-CDs and 1.6 ± 0.4 nm for D-Cys-CDs. A similar typical graphene lattice of 0.23 nm was observed in their high-resolution TEM images (Fig. 1B and C, Insets), indicating their graphite-like core structure.

Unlike the absorption band of L/D-Cys at 210 nm, both L/D-Cys-CDs possessed the UV absorption centering at 244 nm and 350 nm (Fig. 1D), which was due to the π–π* conjugation of sp2-hybridized carbon in the carbonized core of CDs, and the n-π* transition from CO or C–N, respectively. In addition, both L/D-Cys-CDs emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 1E and F, Insets), and the maximum emission occurred at 425 nm under 350 nm maximum excitation (Fig. 1E and F); conversely, neither L-Cys nor D-Cys emitted the fluorescence. The prominent fluorescence of L/D-Cys-CDs could result from the recombination of electrons and holes in them, and the presence of electron-donating groups such as –OH and –NH2 as characterized below [34].

FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2 A revealed that L/D-Cys-CDs shared the same functional groups, e.g. O–H/N–H (3441 cm−1), C–H (2927 cm−1), CO (1731 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1), O–H (1417 cm−1) and C–O (1156 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1), which mainly originated from their raw materials, i.e. CA, L/D-Cys and β-cyclodextrin. Compared with β-cyclodextrin, the –OH vibration peak of L/D-Cys-CDs was slightly blue-shifted, from 3471 cm−1 to 3436 cm−1 in L-Cys-CDs and to 3456 cm−1 in D-Cys-CDs, respectively, probably due to the formation of hydrogen bonds during carbonization.

XPS measurements were conducted to analyze the surface elemental composition and functional groups of Cys-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, four identical significant peaks but with varied intensity were found in the full scan spectra, i.e. S (163.4 eV, 0.8 %), C (285.9 eV, 53.5 %), N (399.6 eV, 1.0 %) and O (532.3 eV, 44.7 %) for L-Cys-CDs, while S (163.4 eV, 0.74 %), C (285.9 eV, 61.69 %), N (399.7 eV, 2.56 %) and O (532.3 eV, 34.99 %) for D-Cys-CDs. L/D-Cys-CDs both possessed the highest content of C and the lowest content of S. Compared with L-Cys-CDs, D-Cys-CDs had an elevated N content up to 2.56 % and a reduced O content of 34.99 %. In addition, as depicted in Fig. 2C, three identical peaks, i.e. -C-CO (287.1 eV), C–O/C–N/C–S (288.9 eV) and C–C (284.1 eV), were present in the C1s spectra of both L-/D-Cys-CDs; a distinctive peak at 289.0 eV (-O-CO) appeared for D-Cys-CDs but not for L-Cys-CDs. Otherwise, both L-Cys-CDs and D-Cys-CDs shared the same O1s, N1s and S2p spectra (Fig. 2D, E and 2F). As discussed in Section 3.1, the interactions among the ternary carbon sources differed for L/D-Cys, and thus esterification or amidation during the formation of CDs may also change, which may be reflected by the distinct -O-CO and elevated N content in D-Cys-CDs [35,36].

From the above analysis, L/D-Cys-CDs almost owned similar morphology with comparable size and shared the same kind of surface groups, such as –COOH, –OH, –NH2, –CO–NH-, –SH and –NH2, which may lay the foundation for their multiple functions. However, the element content was different. Especially, the N content of D-Cys-CDs (2.56 %) was higher than that of L-Cys-CDs (1.0 %), which might contribute to their different affinities for Cu2+ or Aβ42-Cu2+ as disclosed by K b (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inhibitory effect of Cys-CDs on Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: According to the metal dyshomeostasis hypothesis, Cu2+ is highly associated with the aggregation of Aβ, and inhibition of Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation might be potential for AD therapy [37]. On the other hand, the aggregation of pure Aβ is highly influenced by the chiral microenvironment. Hence, the effect of L/D-Cys-CDs on pure Aβ42 aggregation and Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation was separately studied as below.

It has been claimed that the β-sheet structure and morphology of Aβ aggregates is highly related to the molar ratio of Aβ to Cu2+. To obtain the fibrils of Aβ42-Cu2+, (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5 was used as previously reported [38,39]. According to the aggregation kinetics of pure Aβ42 monitored by the ThT assay (Fig. 3 A), the fluorescence intensity of the incubation system strengthened with the prolonged incubation time when Aβ42 was incubated alone, implying the formation of β-sheet-rich structure to a high degree (group I, curve I). Compared to the pure Aβ42 aggregation (with the final normalized ThT fluorescence as 100 %), the aggregation of Aβ42 was decreased by 16.16 % in the presence of L-Cys-CDs (group II, curve II), while decreased by 39.20 % of D-Cys-CDs (group III, curve III). This result indicated that D-Cys-CDs affected Aβ42 aggregation more prominently than its enantiomer L-Cys-CDs, disclosing the chirality preference in the inhibition of Aβ42 aggregation. Also, in line with the previous findings [40], the chiral nanoagent with a positive cotton effect prohibited the aggregation of Aβ more powerfully compared to its enantiomer, i.e. D-Cys-CDs with positive cotton effect in this case (Fig. 1A).

In addition to the inhibition of Cys-CDs on the aggregation of pure Aβ42, Cys-CDs could also take effect in inhibiting Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation in light of their high binding affinity with Cu2+, as illustrated in Fig. 3B. Compared to the maximum ThT fluorescence of (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5 (group IV, curve IV), the Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation was inhibited by 28.90 % (group V, curve V) and 69.37 % (group VI, curve VI) in the respective L-Cys-CDs and D-Cys-CDs, indicating that L-Cys-CDs exerted weaker inhibitory effect than D-Cys-CDs. In addition, compared with the inhibition efficiency towards pure Aβ42 aggregation (Fig. 3A), the L/D-Cys-CDs had further inhibitory effect on Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation by 12.14 % and 30.14 %, respectively. This observation illustrated that the affinity of L/D-Cys-CDs to Cu2+ may assist further inhibition on Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation than pure Aβ aggregation, and D-Cys-CDs behaved much better.

To confirm the effects of Cys-CDs on Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregates, the final products of group I, IV, V, and VI were observed by TEM. As depicted in Fig. 3C, the typical amyloid fibrils were found for pure Aβ42 with a length of about several hundred nanometers. When incubated with Cu2+, substantial fibrils can be observed (Fig. 3D). Even in the case of (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5 + L-Cys-CDs, some aggregates were still present (Fig. 3E); however, the mature fibril-like structure was seldom present but replaced by amorphous aggregates in the case of (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5 + D-Cys-CDs (Fig. 3F), demonstrating that the fibrillation of (Aβ42-Cu2+)2.5 was largely suppressed by D-Cys-CDs.

To better understand the enantiomeric effect on inhibition of Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation, docking studies were performed. The NMR monomeric Cu-Aβ1-23 complex (PDB, 8B9R) was used as a host structure, since the 12–23 segment (HHQKLVFFAED) is the critical region responsible for Aβ aggregation [41]. Additionally, as discussed in Sections 3.1 and 3.2, L/D-Cys-CDs retained some functional groups of L/D-Cys, but had no definite structure. Thus, L/D-Cys instead of L/D-Cys-CDs were adopted for simulation. The best fit was shown in Fig. 3G and H. The Asp1, Glu11 and Phe20 were identified as the binding sites of both L/D-Cys with Cu-Aβ1-23, but two additional hydrogen binding sites were identified for D-Cys, i.e. Gly9 and His13. The carbonyl group in D-Cys was closer to the hydroxyl group of Gly9, allowing the formation of a hydrogen bond; however, the carbonyl group in L-Cys could not form such a hydrogen bond with Gly9, possibly because of the distance between them. For His13, a similar difference between L/D-Cys was observed, as the sulfhydryl group in D-Cys was close to the hydroxyl group of His13. In consideration of that L/D-Cys-CDs retained respective chiral centers of L/D-Cys, the different interactions between L/D-Cys-CDs and Cu-Aβ1-23 complex may still exist. From this, chiral discrimination may be expected between L/D-Cys-CDs and Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregates. It can be concluded that chiral discrimination and Cu2+ binding affinity could both contribute to the inhibition on Cu2+-induced Aβ aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scavenging free radicals by Cys-CDs and regulation effects of Cys-CDs on the POD-like activity of Aβ42-Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots can serve as effective electron transporters and attach to highly reactive free radicals, thus to scavenge free radicals and reduce oxidative stress in AD, exerting a protective effect [42]. The total antioxidant properties of Cys-CDs were estimated by the ABTS+• assay. As discussed in Section 3.1, L/D-Cys-CDs both behaved well in the elimination of ABTS+• (Table S1).

Moreover, the ability of Cys-CDs to scavenge the two prevalent ROS, i.e. •OH and O2 •-, was particularly studied. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, L/D-Cys-CDs both performed high comparable scavenge efficiency toward •OH in the concentration-dependent manner. The elimination rates of L/D-Cys-CDs at the concentration of 600 μg/mL towards •OH reached 52.4 % and 53.4 %, respectively, with a similar EC50 value of about 500 μg/mL. Additionally, D-Cys-CDs exhibited a significant scavenging ability towards O2 •-, with the elimination rate of 57.0 % at the concentration of 600 μg/mL (Fig. 4B), and the EC50 value was measured as about 200 μg/mL. As a contrast, L-Cys-CDs did not exhibit obvious O2 •- scavenging ability at the comparable concentration to D-Cys-CDs. Based on these results, D-Cys-CDs possessed excellent scavenging ability to eliminate ABTS+•, •OH and O2 •-; while, L-Cys-CDs can only scavenge ABTS+• and •OH, but not O2 •-. The variability in radical scavenging ability of these two CDs may be caused by the difference of the surface groups. More specifically, D-Cys-CDs possessed remarkable -O-CO (Fig. 2C), which was supposed to combine with O2 •- through weak interactions such as electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bond, etc [43].

As a transition redox metal cation, Cu2+ can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to release •OH which manifests POD-like catalytic ability [44]. This POD-like catalytic property would be further strengthened once Cu2+ is bound to Aβ, leading to serious cellular oxidative damage [7]. Inspired by the affinity of Cys-CDs towards Cu2+, especially D-Cys-CDs (K
b = 3.05 × 104 M−1), we suggested that they may inhibit the POD-like activity of Cu2+ to alleviate oxidative stress.

Thereby to regulate the POD-like catalytic ability of Cu2+ by L/D-Cys-CDs, the TMB assay was carried out to examine whether L/D-Cys-CDs could retard the oxidization of TMB catalyzed by Cu2+. Firstly, L/D-Cys-CDs did not have POD activity itself, as the presence of L/D-Cys-CDs could not arouse the oxidation of TMB (Fig. S8). It was noteworthy that the oxidization of TMB was undetectable in the absence of Cu2+, but highly accelerated within 30 min following the addition of Cu2+, suggesting the catalytic role of Cu2+ (Fig. 4C). However, with the further addition of Cys-CDs, the oxidation of TMB was inhibited. Compared to the L-Cys-CDs treated group, the more weakened absorbance at A652 in the case of D-Cys-CDs was observed, implying the superior inhibition ability of D-Cys-CDs to L-Cys-CDs towards POD-like activity of Cu2+. The same phenomenon was also observed in the experimental group of (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (Fig. 4D). In the presence of (Aβ42-Cu2+)1, a significant absorption at 652 nm was observed, confirming the similar POD-like activity of (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 to Cu2+. D-Cys-CDs can interact with (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 to inhibit its POD-like catalytic ability, exhibiting a better regulation effect than its enantiomer L-Cys-CDs. The regulation of D/L-Cys-CDs on POD activity of Cu2+ may be resulted from the direct interaction with Cu2+ and the elimination of •OH produced by Cu2+. D/L-Cys-CDs had comparable scavenging ability towards •OH but different binding affinity with Cu2+, which may lead to discrepancy in the regulation effect on the POD activity of Cu2+.

The accumulation of Aβ42 in extracellular is presumed to be the cause of AD damage. However, it has been also well documented that the reuptake of Aβ42 into neurons may lead to the intracellular aggregation of Aβ42, thus resulting in neurotoxicity [45]. So, in many attempts to cure AD, the cellular uptake was studied for effective therapy [46,47]. In our study, it is also important for CDs to enter cells to exhibit the inhibitory effect on Aβ42 aggregation. Herein, the cell uptake experiment was performed. As photographed in confocal fluorescence microscopic images, the bright red fluorescence was observed in both cases of L/D-Cys-CDs-Cy5, which were mainly distributed in the cytoplasm, indicating the successful internalization of them in SH-SY5Y cells (Fig. 5 A). As depicted in Fig. 5B, compared to the control group, the mean fluorescence intensity of L/D-Cys-CDs-Cy5 in SH-SY5Y cells was up to a significant level. Therein, the emission intensity of L/D-Cys-CDs-Cy5 treated cells were 56.1 % and 83.5 % higher than the control group, respectively, disclosing that D-Cys-CDs-Cy5 was internalized better than L-Cys-CDs-Cy5 in SH-SY5Y cells. The differentiation in the cellular uptake behavior could be due to the chirality preference, which made D-Cys-CDs a more efficient nanoagent for applications in cell and in vivo [48]. Meanwhile, the successful internalization in cells also represented the good biocompatibility of CDs, which is beneficial for them to exert their biological effect.

The pathology of neurodegenerative diseases is closely related to the process of chronic neuroinflammation. Inflammatory factors may contribute to nerve cell death and accelerate the process of neurodegenerative diseases. Among those, NFKBIA is phosphorylated by IκB kinase (IKK) and can release NF-κB when the relative pathway is activated [49]. Also, interleukin-6 (IL-6) and interleukin-8 (IL-8) may interplay with Aβ42 in AD-related pathology [50,51]. When (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 was chosen as the stimulator for the inflammatory response in SH-SY5Y cells, the levels of NFKBIA (1.81-fold) (Fig. 5C), IL-8 (2.58-fold) (Fig. 5D) and IL-6 (2.86-fold) (Fig. 5E) were elevated. L/D-Cys-CDs both suppressed these mRNA levels in SH-SY5Y cells treated with (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 to some extent. However, D-Cys-CDs can reduce the mRNA level of IL-6 genes more prominently than L-Cys-CDs, i.e. 1.01-fold of the control group for D-Cys-CDs-treated cells and 1.50-fold of the control group for L-Cys-CDs-treated cells. Both L/D-Cys-CDs can protect SH-SY5Y cells from (Aβ42-Cu2+)1-induced neuroinflammation by reducing the mRNA level of NFKBIA, IL-6 and IL-8 genes. Particularly, D-Cys-CDs had better efficiency in reducing mRNA levels of IL-6 than L-Cys-CDs, possibly demonstrating a better anti-inflammation effect of D-Cys-CDs.

As deleterious ROS generation is one of the crucial mediators in the pathogenesis of AD and Cys-CDs exhibited good ROS scavenging ability in vitro (Section 3.1, 3.4), Cys-CDs were further examined for scavenging intracellular ROS. DCFH-DA, which can penetrate the cell and be oxidized by free radicals to generate DCF with turn-on green fluorescence, was applied to evaluate intracellular ROS levels. As imaged in Fig. 6 A, distinct green fluorescence was observed in SH-SY5Y cells treated by (Aβ42-Cu2+)1, suggesting the generation of abundant ROS. The highly cytotoxic (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 complex may lead to cellular oxidative damage through the generation of various ROS [52]. Encouragingly, compared to cells only treated with (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 complex, L-Cys-CDs + (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 and D-Cys-CDs + (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 gained 18 % and 50 % fluorescence reduction (Fig. 6B), respectively, indicating the less production of intracellular ROS. More fluorescence reduction was obtained in the case of D-Cys-CDs, suggesting its better ability to scavenge intracellular ROS. Considering that L/D-Cys-CDs had comparable antioxidant ability (Section 3.1, Fig. S4) and •OH scavenging ability (Section 3.4, Fig. 4A), this differential intracellular ROS scavenging ability could be resulted from different regulation effects towards the POD-like activity of Aβ42-Cu2+ (Section 3.4, Fig. 4D).

To further study the scavenging mechanism of intracellular ROS by Cys-CDs, H2O2 was also used as the other stimulator to produce intracellular ROS. Similarly, H2O2 can produce ROS in SH-SY5Y cells (Fig. 6C). However, both L/D-Cys-CDs demonstrated almost comparable efficacy in reducing the ROS generated by H2O2, resulting in a reduction of DCF fluorescence intensity to 82 % and 73 %, respectively (Fig. 6D).

Obviously, D-Cys-CDs exhibited almost equivalent antioxidant activity to L-Cys-CDs in the case of H2O2 as the stimulant but displayed distinct scavenging ability against intracellular ROS induced by (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 (Fig. 6B). As discussed in Section 3.1 (Fig. S4), both L/D-Cys-CDs could scavenge ROS equally possibly owing to their comparable reducibility. Also, L/D-Cys-CDs had comparable •OH scavenging ability (Section 3.4, Fig. 4A). However, D-Cys-CDs regulated the POD-like activity of (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 more effectively (Section 3.4, Fig. 4D), which would assist to promote its ability to scavenge ROS. This observation provided further evidence that the regulation of the POD-like activity of (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 may represent a key mechanism for mitigating excess ROS produced by this complex. As a comprehensive result, the reduction of intracellular ROS was more remarkable for D-Cys-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alleviation of the intracellular cytotoxicity induced by Aβ42-Cu2+ complex using Cys-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The alleviation of cytotoxicity induced by (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 was further investigated. Firstly, Cys-CDs barely affected the cell viability of SH-SY5Y cells at 100–1000 μg/mL concentrations with viability >95 % (Fig. 7 A), demonstrating their favorable bio-compatibility. However, when (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 was incubated with SH-SY5Y cells, cell viability went down to 54 % because of the significant cytotoxicity of the complex (Fig. 7B). Encouragingly, when cells were co-incubated with both (Aβ42-Cu2+)1 and L/D-Cys-CDs, their viabilities were gradually recovered to 96 % and 114 %, respectively. Obviously, both D-Cys-CDs and L-Cys-CDs can effectively alleviate the cytotoxicity of the Aβ42-Cu2+ complex with the stronger effect of the former.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of Cys-CDs on Caenorhabditis elegans CL2120
ce:section.ce:para: Transgenic Caenorhabditis elegans is a representative in vivo model, and has been widely used for AD studies [53,54]. To test the protection ability of Cys-CDs for AD model animals, AD transgenic Caenorhabditis elegans CL2120 which expresses green fluorescence protein (GFP) labelled human Aβ42 in muscle cells were herein used as the in vivo model (Section 2.11) [55]. The full-length Aβ42 protein is consistently expressed and subsequently forms aggregates in the body wall muscle cells, resulting in the occurrence of paralysis during adulthood.

As imaged in Fig. 8A–I, the Aβ42 deposit was largely accumulated on the 3rd day of adulthood with the presence of green spots in the body wall of worms. When CL2120 was only treated with Cys-CDs, the Aβ42 deposit could mostly diminish in either L-Cys-CDs or D-Cys-CDs based on the weakened fluorescence intensity (Fig. 8A–II and 8A-III). When CL2120 was treated with Cu2+ (Fig. 8A–IV), the Aβ42 deposit was more conspicuous compared with the control group, indicating that Cu2+ can deteriorate the Aβ42 deposit in CL2120. However, when treated with Cys-CDs (Fig. 8A–V and 8A-VI), the green spots at the head region can be largely suppressed, and more obviously for D-Cys-CDs, as reflected by the weakened fluorescence intensity (Fig. 8 B). From the observations, we can see that L/D-Cys-CDs performed almost equally in inhibiting the pure Aβ42 aggregation in CL2120, but D-Cys-CDs performed better than its enantiomer in inhibiting Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation. These observations agreed well with the above results that D-Cys-CDs can better inhibit Cu2+-induced Aβ42 aggregation (Fig. 3B), which was attributed to the enantiomeric effect of chiral CDs.

The accumulation and deposition of Aβ42 in body wall muscle cells could lead to paralysis or even death of the worms. As reflected in Fig. 8C, the paralysis rate of the control group (group a) elevated steadily from 0 % to 36.4 % during the observation. However, when treated with L/D-Cys-CDs (group c, e), the paralysis rate was controlled under 15 %. Compared with the control group (group a), the Cu2+ treated group (group b) showed accelerated paralysis with the paralysis rate up to 51.6 % on the 9th day. As a contrast, L/D-Cys-CDs + Cu2+ group (group d, f) showed deferred paralysis by the 6th day. The paralysis rate was significantly lowered to 6.4 % on the 9th day in the D-Cys-CDs + Cu2+ group (group f) compared to 10.0 % in the L-Cys-CDs + Cu2+ group (group d), suggesting distinct protection effect of D-Cys-CDs on AD nematode.

In summary, multifunctional chiral carbon dots based on ternary carbon sources, L/d-cysteine, β-cyclodextrin and citric acid, were designed and prepared. β-cyclodextrin played important multiple roles to induce chirality, enhance Cu2+ binding ability, and promote the antioxidant ability of the resulting CDs, particularly D-Cys-CDs. D-Cys-CDs with multi-functionalities, as well as multiple chiral centers, can interact effectively with Cu2+ and thereby modulate its biological activity, including its regulation on Aβ aggregation and POD-like activity of Cu2+. Owing to its chirality, D-Cys-CDs showed more excellent performance in cell uptake than L-Cys-CDs with better anti-inflammation ability and enhanced alleviation ability of cytotoxicity in SH-SY5Y cells. D-Cys-CDs succeeded in diminishing Aβ plaques and relieving paralysis of CL2120 AD nematode. The present study represents the first endeavor, to the best of our knowledge, in exploring the potential of chiral CDs for Cu2+-induced AD therapy. The utilization of β-cyclodextrin as a chirality inducer with anticipated multiple functions for constructing CDs could be a novel strategy to construct chiral nanoagent, thereby enhancing therapeutic efficacy and providing theragnostic properties.
Data will be made available on request.
